The surrounding gingiva, the floor of the mouth, and the lingual mucosa are also visible. A retractor edge is faintly seen at the bottom.

**3. Condition / clinical findings:** There is heavy supragingival and subgingival calculus (tartar) deposition along the lingual surfaces of the lower anterior teeth, appearing as thick yellow-brown mineralized accumulations concentrated near the gingival margins—a typical location given proximity to the sublingual salivary gland ducts. Brown extrinsic staining is also visible on the tooth surfaces. The gingiva appears reddened and somewhat swollen along the margins, suggesting associated gingival inflammation (gingivitis). The posterior tooth shows occlusal staining/possible early decay in the fissures. Overall this represents moderate to heavy calculus buildup with poor oral hygiene; precise periodontal severity (attachment loss) cannot be determined from a photograph alone.
judge1_notes: Accurate identification of lingual mandibular calculus with correct modality and no prohibited mislabeling; severity is not applicable per ground truth.

description: This is an intraoral clinical photograph showing the lingual (tongue-side) view of the mandibular anterior teeth and adjacent left premolars. Visible structures include the teeth, the lingual gingiva, the floor of the mouth, and the retracted lower lip. The primary clinical finding is a severe accumulation of yellowish-white dental calculus (tartar) heavily coating the cervical thirds and interproximal spaces of the anterior teeth. Additionally, there is prominent brown extrinsic staining on the lingual surfaces of the crowns, mild crowding of the incisors, and the adjacent marginal gingiva appears erythematous (reddened), indicating inflammation secondary to the heavy calculus deposits.
judge1_notes: Accurate identification of lingual mandibular calculus with correct modality; severity is not applicable per ground truth.

description: This is a clinical intraoral photograph presenting an occlusal view of the mandibular (lower) dental arch. The visible anatomy includes the mandibular anterior teeth (incisors and canines) and the left posterior teeth (premolars and molars), surrounded by the gingiva and the floor of the mouth. The image displays significant clinical findings indicative of poor oral hygiene and potential periodontal disease. There is heavy accumulation of supragingival calculus (tartar), appearing as thick, yellowish-white deposits at the cervical margins (gumline) and between the lower front teeth, particularly the central incisors. Additionally, there is widespread brown/orange extrinsic staining on the labial and lingual surfaces of the teeth, characteristic of tobacco use or chromogenic dietary habits. The gingival tissue shows signs of recession, and there appears to be partial edentulism (missing teeth) in the right posterior quadrant (far left of the image). The severity of the calculus buildup suggests chronic neglect and likely underlying gingivitis or periodontitis.
judge1_notes: Correctly identifies clinical photo, lingual mandibular anterior view, and heavy calculus deposits without major errors.

description: The image is a clinical photograph of the lower anterior teeth. The visible anatomy includes the mandibular incisors and canines, as well as the surrounding gingiva. The teeth appear to be covered in a significant amount of yellowish-brown calculus, particularly on the lingual surfaces, with some visible staining. The gingiva appears inflamed and shiny, indicating possible gingivitis. The presence of heavy calculus suggests poor oral hygiene, which may be contributing to the gingival inflammation.
judge1_notes: Accurate identification of clinical photo, lingual mandibular anterior surfaces, and heavy yellow-brown calculus with no errors.

description: This is a clinical intraoral photograph of the maxillary anterior teeth. Visible structures include the maxillary incisors and canines, and the surrounding gingival mucosa. The teeth exhibit prominent brown discoloration on their labial surfaces, with no obvious severe gingival inflammation. The discoloration affects multiple anterior teeth, indicating a widespread staining or demineralization process.
judge1_notes: Correctly identifies clinical photo but mislabels as maxillary/labial and calls deposits staining/demineralization rather than calculus.

description: This is a labeled anatomical illustration/cross-sectional diagram of a tooth. It shows the crown, neck, and root, with enamel covering the crown, dentin beneath it, a central pulp chamber/root canal containing blood vessels and nerves, gingiva around the cervical area, cementum over the root, and surrounding jaw bone with vascular structures. No disease, pathology, or abnormal clinical finding is depicted; it is a normal tooth anatomy diagram.
judge1_notes: Accurate description covering all labeled structures, regions, and correctly identifying healthy anatomy with no errors.

description:
**1. Type of image:** This is an educational anatomical diagram/illustration — a cross-sectional (sagittal) view of a tooth with labeled structures.

**2. Anatomy and structures visible:** The illustration shows a multi-rooted tooth in longitudinal section embedded in the surrounding tissues. Labeled components include: the **enamel** (outer white/bluish crown layer, shown with a stippled/cell-like texture), the **dentin** (orange layer beneath the enamel forming the bulk of the tooth), the **pulp chamber** containing blood vessels (red/blue) and nerves (yellow), the **cementum** (covering the root surface), the **gingiva** (orange soft tissue around the neck), and the **jaw bone** (alveolar bone shown with trabecular/spongy texture surrounding the roots). The tooth is divided into three regions on the right: **crown**, **neck**, and **root**. Blood vessels (red artery, blue vein/nerve) are shown entering at the root apex.

**3. Condition/pathology:** No pathology, disease, or clinical abnormality is depicted. This is a normal, healthy reference illustration of tooth anatomy intended for teaching purposes.
judge1_notes: Accurate, complete description covering all required structures, regions, and correctly identifying healthy anatomy in a diagram.

description: This is a labeled, cross-sectional anatomical illustration of a multi-rooted tooth and its surrounding structures. The visible anatomy is divided into the crown, neck, and root regions, specifically depicting and labeling the enamel, dentin, pulp chamber (containing blood vessels and nerves), cementum, gingiva, and jaw bone. The image depicts healthy, normal dental anatomy with intact tissues and no visible pathology, decay, or signs of disease.
judge1_notes: Accurate description covering all required points with correct modality and healthy anatomy.

description: This is a labeled anatomical cross-sectionalillustration of a human tooth, likely a molar, embedded in the jaw. The diagram clearly identifies key structures including the outer white Enamel, the underlying yellowish Dentin, the central Pulp chamber containing blood vessels (red and blue lines) and nerves (yellow lines), the Cementum covering the root, and the surrounding Gingiva (gums) and spongy Jaw bone. Brackets on the right side delineate the three main regions of the tooth: the Crown, Neck, and Root. The image depicts a healthy, normal tooth anatomy with no visible pathology, decay, or disease; all tissues appear intact and structurally sound, serving as an educational reference for dental anatomy.
judge1_notes: Accurate and complete description covering all required anatomical structures and regions without errors.

description: The image is a diagram of a tooth, illustrating its various anatomical structures. The visible anatomy includes the enamel, dentin, gingiva, pulp chamber with blood vessels and nerves, cementum, and jawbone. The diagram also labels different parts of the tooth, such as the crown, neck, and root. There are no visible conditions, pathologies, or clinical findings depicted in the image; it appears to be a normal tooth anatomy illustration.

description:

description: This is a schematic dental anatomy diagram showing longitudinal and transverse sections of a posterior tooth. Visible structures include the crown with an outer enamel layer, underlying dentin, a central pulp chamber with root canals, two roots, and a thin root surface layer consistent with cementum/periodontal covering. The transverse section shows dentin surrounding two pulp canal openings. No disease, caries, fracture, bone loss, or other pathology is depicted.

description:
**1. Type of image:** This is a black-and-white line diagram (illustration), not a photograph or radiograph. It shows two cross-sectional views of a tooth—a longitudinal (vertical) section labeled "1" and a transverse (horizontal/cross) section labeled "2."

**2. Anatomy and structures visible:** The diagram depicts a multi-rooted (two-rooted) posterior tooth in cut section. Labeled structures include:
- **A:** The outermost layer over the crown, hatched, representing **enamel**.
- **D:** The thick hatched layer making up the bulk of the tooth body, representing **dentin**.
- **B:** The central hollow space—the **pulp cavity/chamber** in section 1, and the **pulp canals (root canals)** seen as two small openings in the transverse section 2.
- **C:** The thin stippled outer layer along the root surface, representing **cementum**.

The longitudinal section shows the crown, the pulp chamber extending into the divided roots, and the canal spaces within each root. The transverse section shows the layered arrangement of enamel/dentin surrounding the pulp canals in cross-section.

**3. Condition or pathology:** No pathology, disease, or clinical abnormality is depicted. This is a normal anatomical/educational diagram illustrating tooth structure and the relationship of hard tissues (enamel, dentin, cementum) to the internal pulp space.
judge1_notes: Accurate identification of the schematic diagram, both sectional views, the A-D letter labels, and correct interpretation as normal anatomy without inventing names in the image.

description: This is a black-and-white anatomical line drawing depicting two cross-sectional views of a multi-rooted tooth (view 1 is longitudinal, view 2 is transverse). The diagram uses letters to label the primary structural layers of the tooth: "A" points to the enamel (the striated outer layer of the crown), "B" indicates the pulp cavity (the central chamber and canals), "C" points to the cementum (the stippled outer layer of the roots), and "D" represents the dentin (the bulk middle layer with radiating lines). The image illustrates normal, healthy dental anatomy with intact, uniform layers and no visible pathology, disease, or structural defects.
judge1_notes: Accurate description covering all required points; correctly identifies line drawing, both views, letter labels, and normal anatomy without inventing disease.

description:
Based on the visual evidence:

1.  **Type of image:** This is a schematic line drawing or educational diagram illustrating dental anatomy. It presents two views: a longitudinal (vertical) section labeled "1" and a transverse (horizontal) cross-section labeled "2".

2.  **Anatomy and structures visible:** The image depicts a two-rooted tooth, likely a mandibular molar.
    *   **Figure 1 (Longitudinal section):** Shows the internal structure of the tooth within its socket. The central white area labeled **B** represents the pulp cavity (pulp chamber and root canals). The surrounding hatched layer labeled **D** is dentin. The outermost hatched layers labeled **A** (top) and **C** (bottom/sides) represent the surrounding periodontal structures, likely the gingiva/alveolar bone and the periodontal ligament/cementum interface, respectively.
    *   **Figure 2 (Transverse section):** Shows a cross-section, likely through the root (specifically a mesial root given the two canals). The outer layer **A** represents the periodontium/cementum. The thick layer with radiating lines **D** represents dentin with dentinal tubules. The two small circles labeled **B** represent the root canals (neurovascular bundles), indicating a multi-canaled root system.

3.  **Condition/Pathology:** The image depicts **normal tooth anatomy**. There are no signs of pathology such as dental caries (cavities), fractures, periapical abscesses, or significant bone loss. It appears to be a standard anatomical illustration used to demonstrate the relationship between the pulp, dentin, and surrounding periodontal tissues. The narrowness of the root canals in Figure 1 suggests an adult tooth with some secondary dentin deposition, which is a normal physiological finding.
judge1_notes: Correctly identifies schematic line drawing, both sectional views, letter labels, and normal anatomy without naming structures in image text.

description: The image is a diagram illustrating the anatomy of a tooth. The visible anatomy includes the enamel (A), pulp chamber and root canal with visible accessory canals (B), cementum (C), and dentin (D). No condition, pathology, or clinical finding is depicted in this image; it appears to be a normal tooth anatomy illustration.
judge1_notes: Correctly identifies the diagram as normal tooth anatomy, but assigns specific structure names to letter labels and omits the two-view (longitudinal/transverse) sectional description.

description: This is a diagram (longitudinal and cross - sectional illustration) of a tooth. Visible anatomy includes enamel (A, outer layer), dentin (D, middle layer), pulp chamber (B, central cavity with pulp), and root (C, lower portion). The cross - section (2) shows the pulp chamber and root canals (B). No pathology is visible; the image depicts normal tooth structure with intact enamel, dentin, and pulp.
judge1_notes: Correctly identifies diagram and two views with letter labels, but violates the must_avoid by naming structures in words rather than noting labels are letters only.

description: This is a labeled anatomical illustration/cross-section of a multi-rooted tooth (likely a molar). It shows the crown with white enamel, underlying orange dentin, a pink pulp chamber containing red and blue neurovascular structures, root canals extending to the apices, surrounding gingival tissue, periodontal ligament, cementum/root surface, and alveolar bone. No disease, caries, fracture, periapical lesion, or periodontal bone loss is depicted; it appears to be a normal tooth anatomy diagram.
judge1_notes: Accurate description covering all required anatomical points without prohibited errors; severity is not applicable for this diagram.

description:
This is a labeled anatomical diagram (illustration) showing a cross-section of a molar tooth within its supporting bone, presented in a schematic, cartoon style.

**Anatomy/structures visible (with the labeled numbers):**
- **1** – Enamel (the white outer covering of the crown).
- **2** – Dentin (the orange bulk of the tooth beneath the enamel).
- **3** – Pulp chamber/pulp (the pink coronal pulp containing red and blue vessels).
- **4** – Gingiva/gum tissue.
- **5** – Root canals and root dentin (containing the neurovascular bundle running down the multi-rooted tooth).
- **6** – Surrounding alveolar bone.
- **7 and 8** – Blood vessels (an artery shown in red and a vein in blue at the base).
- **9** – Bracket indicating the crown region.
- **10** – Bracket indicating the root region.

The diagram also depicts the periodontal/vascular supply, with red (arterial) and blue (venous) vessels entering through the root apices and branching into the pulp.

**Condition/pathology:** This is a normal, healthy anatomical reference illustration—no caries, fracture, periapical lesion, or other pathology is depicted. Its purpose is educational labeling of tooth structures rather than showing disease.
judge1_notes: Accurately identifies the diagram, all numbered structures, vessels, crown/root brackets, and correctly notes normal anatomy with no pathology.

description: This is an anatomical illustration depicting a cross-section of a healthy, multi-rooted tooth and its surrounding periodontium. Visible structures include the enamel (white outer layer of the crown), dentin (orange middle layer), and the pulp chamber and root canals containing blood vessels (indicated by red and blue lines). The tooth is supported by the gingiva (pink tissue), alveolar bone (tan tissue), and the periodontal ligament space. Blank leader lines point to these specific anatomical features for educational labeling. There is no visible pathology, decay, or disease shown; the image represents normal, healthy dental anatomy.
judge1_notes: Accurate description of the labeled molar cross-section with correct identification of structures, vessels, and blank leader lines.

description:
Based on the image provided:

1.  **Type of image:** This is a labeled anatomical diagram or illustration showing a cross-sectional view of a tooth and its surrounding structures.
2.  **Anatomy and structures visible:** The image depicts a molar tooth embedded in the jaw. Key structures include the enamel (1), the underlying dentin (2), and the central pulp chamber containing nerves and blood vessels (3). The gingiva or gum tissue (4) surrounds the neck of the tooth. The roots contain root canals with neurovascular bundles (5) and are surrounded by the periodontal ligament and cementum, set within the alveolar bone (6). At the base, major blood vessels (artery and vein, 7 and 8) are visible. Brackets 9 and 10 demarcate the clinical crown and root, respectively.
3.  **Condition/Pathology:** The diagram depicts a healthy tooth with no visible pathology. There are no signs of dental caries (cavities), fractures, or significant periodontal bone loss. The large pulp chamber suggests a young, vital tooth. The image serves as a standard educational model of normal dental anatomy.
judge1_notes: Accurate diagram description but incorrectly claims structures are named in words when only numbers appear.

description: The image is a diagram of a tooth and its surrounding structures. The visible anatomy includes the tooth crown (1), enamel, dentin (2), gingiva (3), interdental gingiva (4), periodontal ligament (5), root (6), root apex (7), and surrounding bone with blood vessels (8). The diagram also labels the visible portion of the tooth and surrounding tissue as (9) and the root portion as (10). There are no visible conditions, pathologies, or clinical findings depicted in the image.

description:

description: This is a dental illustration/diagram showing occlusal views of maxillary and mandibular dental arches. The left pair appears to depict smaller arches with fewer teeth, and the right pair larger arches with more posterior teeth; visible structures include incisors, canines, premolars/molars, occlusal grooves, gingival margins, palate with rugae, and mandibular arch form. No caries, periodontal disease, fracture, malposition severe enough to identify, or other pathology is clearly depicted.

description:
**1. Type of image:** This is an educational anatomical illustration/diagram comparing the dental arches, with labeled occlusal (biting-surface) views of the upper and lower jaws.

**2. Anatomy and structures visible:** The diagram shows four dental arch outlines, organized as a comparison between two dentitions for both the maxilla (upper, top) and mandible (lower, bottom). The smaller arches (left) depict the primary/deciduous dentition with fewer teeth, while the larger arches (right) depict the permanent/adult dentition with a full complement. Labeled structures include the palate (on the maxillary arches) and individual tooth types: central incisor, lateral incisor, canine, first premolar, second premolar, first molar, second molar, and third molar. The teeth are shown in gray with the occlusal anatomy/cusps and arch-shaped gingival/palatal soft tissue depicted in pink.

**3. Condition/pathology:** No disease, pathology, or clinical abnormality is depicted. This is a normal anatomical reference diagram illustrating tooth nomenclature and the difference between the smaller (fewer teeth) and larger (full adult) dental arches.
judge1_notes: Accurate, complete description covering both dentitions, all labeled tooth types, palate, and correct diagram modality.

description: This is an anatomical diagram illustrating occlusal views of the maxillary (upper) and mandibular (lower) dental arches. It compares the primary dentition (smaller arches on the left) with the permanent dentition (larger arches on the right). Visible structures include the palate and the full complement of teeth for both dentitions, with specific labels identifying the central incisor, lateral incisor, canine, first and second premolars, and first, second, and third molars. No pathology, disease, or abnormal clinical condition is depicted; the image represents normal, healthy dental anatomy and tooth arrangement.

description: This is a schematic dental illustration comparing the primary (deciduous) and permanent dentitions. The image displays occlusal (top-down) views of the dental arches, divided into the **Maxilla** (upper jaw, top row) and **Mandible** (lower jaw, bottom row). The left column shows the smaller primary arches (containing incisors, canines, and molars), while the right column shows the larger permanent arches (containing incisors, canines, premolars, and molars). Specific structures are labeled, including the **Palate**, **Central Incisor**, **Lateral Incisor**, **Canine**, **First/Second/Third Molar**, and **First/Second Premolar**. There is no pathology or disease depicted; the diagram illustrates normal dental anatomy and nomenclature, highlighting the difference in tooth number and type between the two developmental stages.

description:

No condition, pathology, or clinical finding is depicted in the image; it appears to be a normal anatomical representation.

description: This is a schematic dental illustration of a single-rooted tooth in cross-section. It shows the crown and root, the cemento-enamel junction/cervical area, surrounding gingival soft tissue, alveolar bone contour, and periodontal ligament/fiber space. The drawing appears to depict a periodontal pocket or gingival enlargement around the cervical tooth area, with the gingival margin positioned coronally and a measured depth indicated on the right; no radiographic pathology is shown.

description:
**1. Type of image:** This is a schematic line diagram (illustration) of a single tooth in cross-section/longitudinal view, showing its supporting structures.

**2. Anatomy and structures visible:** A single-rooted tooth is depicted with its crown (upper portion outlined in black) and a long tapering root extending downward. Around the root, color-coded structures are shown: a red contour line representing the gingival (gum) margin/soft tissue contour, a brown/ochre line representing the bone (alveolar crest) level, and red hatched/radiating lines between the root surface and the bone outlining the periodontal ligament fibers. A leader line on the right with three dots (a legend marker) points to the area at the crown–root junction (cementoenamel junction/gingival sulcus region).

**3. Condition / clinical finding:** The diagram appears to illustrate the relationship of the gingival margin and the alveolar bone crest to the tooth. The red gingival line sits slightly coronal to the brown bone line, with the periodontal ligament fibers spanning between. As a diagram (not a radiograph or clinical photo), it depicts normal periodontal anatomy / the periodontal attachment apparatus rather than a specific disease. No definitive pathology (such as bone loss with apical migration of the crest) can be confirmed from the schematic alone; the legend dots suggest an instructional emphasis on the gingival sulcus/junctional area.
judge1_notes: Accurately identifies the schematic, all periodontal structures, the three-dot scale, and normal/healthy anatomy without claiming disease.

description: This is a schematic diagram of a single-rooted tooth and its supporting periodontium, illustrating the crown, root, gingiva (red line), alveolar bone (brown line), and periodontal ligament (red hatching). The image depicts a clinical finding where the gingival margin is located coronal to the cementoenamel junction (CEJ), covering a portion of the anatomical crown. A measurement indicator (three black dots between horizontal lines) specifically highlights the distance from the gingival crest to the CEJ, illustrating a condition such as gingival enlargement, altered passive eruption, or a pseudopocket.
judge1_notes: Correctly identifies the schematic, periodontal structures, and scale, but wrongly interprets it as gingival enlargement/pseudopocket rather than a healthy normal sulcus.

description:
Based on the visualevidence, here is the description:

1.  **Type of image:** This is a schematic diagram or medical illustration representing a cross-section of a tooth and its supporting structures.
2.  **Anatomy and structures:** The central white shape represents a tooth with its crown and root. Surrounding the root is a hatched area (red diagonal lines) representing the periodontal ligament space. The brown line outlines the alveolar bone. The red line represents the gingiva (gum tissue). On the right, there are three black dots with horizontal leader lines pointing to specific levels on the tooth/gingiva interface.
3.  **Condition/Pathology:** The image depicts **periodontitis** (gum disease). Key findings include:
    *   **Gingival Inflammation:** The red line representing the gums is swollen and bulbous, indicating inflammation (gingivitis/periodontitis).
    *   **Bone Loss:** The alveolar bone (brown line) has receded significantly down the root (apical migration), exposing the root surface.
    *   **Periodontal Pocket:** There is a deep space or pocket formed between the tooth and the detached gingiva. The red hatching in the periodontal ligament space suggests inflammation or widening of this space.
    *   **Measurements:** The leader lines on the right likely indicate clinical measurements such as probing depth or clinical attachment level, marking the gingival margin and the base of the periodontal pocket.
    *   **Severity:** The condition appears to be **moderate to severe** due to the significant amount of bone loss and the depth of the periodontal pocket shown.
judge1_notes: Correctly identified the schematic, anatomy, and scale markers but wrongly diagnosed it as moderate-to-severe periodontitis with bone loss instead of a healthy periodontium.

description: The image is a diagram illustrating the anatomy of a tooth and its surrounding structures. The visible anatomy includes the tooth, periodontal ligament (red lines within the space between the tooth and the alveolar bone), and alveolar bone (brown lines). The diagram highlights a condition where the alveolar bone has been lost around the tooth, indicated by the three black dots on the right side, suggesting a pathological process such as periodontal disease. The severity of the bone loss is moderate, as it is localized to one area of the tooth.
judge1_notes: Correctly identifies the diagram and periodontal structures but wrongly interprets the depth scale as moderate bone loss/periodontal disease instead of a healthy normal sulcus.

description:

description: This is a schematic cutaway dental illustration of a posterior tooth (molar/premolar-like) in its socket. Visible structures include the crown with enamel, roots, pulp/root canal spaces, surrounding gingiva, periodontal area, and trabecular alveolar bone. A dark brown-gray band of plaque/calculus is shown around the cervical/gingival margin of the tooth, with adjacent red gingival tissue suggesting marginal inflammation. The finding appears to represent heavy cervical plaque/calculus accumulation with gingivitis; no obvious carious cavity or periapical lesion is visible.

description:
**1. Type of image:** This is a 3D illustration/diagram (not a real radiograph or clinical photo) showing a cross-sectional view of a multi-rooted tooth and its supporting structures against a blue background.

**2. Anatomy and structures visible:** The illustration shows a molar/premolar-type tooth with a visible enamel-covered crown, two roots, and the internal pulp canals (the darker channels running through the roots). The supporting tissues are depicted in cross-section: red gingiva (gum) surrounding the cervical region, and the underlying trabecular (cancellous) bone of the alveolar process shown with its porous honeycomb pattern. At the root apex, small red and blue lines represent the neurovascular bundle (artery and vein) entering the tooth.

**3. Condition/clinical finding:** Along the neck of the tooth, at the gumline, there is a band of brownish-tan, rough, irregular deposit encircling the cervical area of the crown/root junction. This appears to represent dental calculus (tartar) and/or plaque accumulation. The gingiva appears slightly bulbous/inflamed in the marginal area. Based solely on what is visible, the finding suggests early periodontal involvement (calculus deposition with possible gingival inflammation), appearing mild to moderate—there is no clear illustration of significant bone loss or root exposure beyond the deposit itself.
judge1_notes: Accurate description covering all required points without over-calling advanced periodontitis or presenting as a real photo.

description: This is a 3D anatomical illustration of a multi-rooted tooth (likely a lower molar) situated within the alveolar bone and gingiva. Visible structures include the tooth crown, roots, cancellous bone, gingival tissue, and apical neurovascular bundles (red and blue lines). The image depicts severe periodontitis, characterized by a heavy, rough band of yellowish-brown calculus (tartar) accumulation at the cervical margin and extending onto the root surface. Associated with this buildup is significant gingival recession and pronounced alveolar bone loss, leaving a substantial portion of the tooth roots exposed above the current bone level.
judge1_notes: Correctly identifies the 3D illustration, anatomy, and cervical calculus, but wrongly over-calls severe periodontitis with marked bone loss and recession when bone support is largely intact.

description: This is a 3D digital illustration showing a cross-sectional view of a two-rooted tooth (likely a premolar or molar) embedded in the jaw. Visible anatomical structures include the tooth crown and roots, the surrounding alveolar bone (depicted with a spongy, cancellous texture), red gingival tissue (gums), and neurovascular bundles (red and blue vessels) entering the root apices. The image depicts a clinical condition characterized by a significant accumulation of brownish, rough-textured dental calculus (tartar) at the cervical margin (gumline). The surrounding gingival tissue appears red and inflamed, suggestive of gingivitis or early periodontitis resulting from this heavy plaque and calculus buildup.
judge1_notes: Accurate identification of the 3D illustration, anatomy, cervical calculus, and gingival inflammation; 'early periodontitis' hedge is minor and bone is intact.

description: The image is a digital illustration of a tooth and its surrounding structures. The visible anatomy includes the tooth, with its crown and two roots, the periodontal ligament, the alveolar bone, and the gingiva. The image shows a significant accumulation of dental calculus or plaque at the cemento-enamel junction, indicating potential periodontal disease. The severity of the condition is not extreme, as there is no visible bone loss or severe gingival inflammation depicted in the image.
judge1_notes: Correctly identifies illustration, anatomy, and cervical calculus, but wrongly denies the visible reddened marginal gingival inflammation/gingivitis.

description:

description: This is a simplified diagram/illustration of a tooth in cross-section. It shows the outer enamel layer, underlying dentin, and central pulp chamber/root canal space. description:
**1. Type of image:** This is a simple hand-drawn line diagram/illustration of a tooth in cross-section, with text labels.

**2. Anatomy and structures visible:** The drawing shows the crown of a posterior tooth (likely a molar, given the multi-cusped occlusal outline). Three structures are labeled: the **enamel** (the outer layer of the crown), the **dentin** (the layer beneath the enamel), and the **pulp** (the central chamber). The overall outline suggests the coronal portion with two cusps and an intercuspal groove at the top.

**3. Condition / clinical finding:** There is a solid black triangular/wedge-shaped area drawn on the right side of the tooth, extending from the outer enamel surface inward toward the dentin. This dark filled-in region appears to depict a **carious lesion (dental caries)** or a defect penetrating the enamel and approaching/reaching the dentin. Based only on what is visible, the lesion appears to extend through the enamel into the dentin layer (moderate depth), but it does not visibly reach the pulp chamber. Severity cannot be precisely determined from a schematic drawing.
judge1_notes: Correctly identifies sketch, labels, and lesion, but overstates depth as reaching dentin rather than enamel-confined.

description: This is a simple line diagram illustrating a cross-section of a tooth crown. The visible and explicitly labeled anatomical structures include the outer enamel layer, the underlying dentin, and the central pulp chamber. A solid black triangular shape on the right lateral surface depicts a carious lesion; the decay originates on the outer enamel surface and extends inward, just penetrating the dentinoenamel junction into the dentin layer. This indicates a moderate stage of caries that has breached the enamel but has not reached the pulp.
judge1_notes: Correctly identifies the line diagram, labels, and lesion, but wrongly claims the caries penetrates into dentin (moderate) when it is confined to enamel (early).

description: This image is a schematic line drawing ordiagram showing a cross-section of a molar tooth. description: The image is a diagram of a tooth. The visible anatomy includes the enamel, dentin, and pulp. description:

Condition/pathology/clinical finding:** A solid black triangular/wedge-shaped area is drawn on the right side of the crown, extending from the outer enamel surface inward through the dentin toward the pulp. description: This is a labeled dental illustration showing root canal treatment. description:
**1. Type of image:** This is a labeled medical illustration/diagram (not a radiograph or clinical photo), depicting a cross-sectional view of a tooth undergoing endodontic (root canal) treatment, titled "Removing Infected Pulp."

**2. Anatomy and structures visible:** The diagram shows a multi-rooted tooth in longitudinal cross-section. Visible structures include the outer enamel (whitish crown layer), dentin (tan/brown bulk of the tooth), the pulp chamber and root canal system (pink/red internal channels), surrounding alveolar bone (yellowish trabecular/spongy bone), and the gingiva (pink soft tissue). A green rubber dam isolates the tooth. An endodontic cleaning file (metal instrument with a blue handle) is inserted into one root canal.

**3. Condition/clinical findings:** The illustration depicts endodontic therapy for infected pulp. At the apex (tip) of one root, there is a brownish/yellow lesion shown, suggesting a periapical/apical lesion (e.g., abscess or granuloma) associated with the infected pulp. The cleaning file is being used to debride and clean the canal. The image illustrates the procedure rather than quantifying disease severity; the apical lesion implies the infection has extended beyond the root tip.
judge1_notes: Accurate description covering diagram type, file, rubber dam, and periapical lesion; severity not applicable.

description: This is a cross-sectional illustration depicting a dental procedure. Visible anatomy includes a multi-rooted tooth (showing enamel, dentin, and root canals), surrounding gingiva, and cancellous alveolar bone. Clinical elements include an access cavity prepared in the crown, a green "Rubber dam" isolating the tooth, and an endodontic "Cleaning file" inserted into the left root canal. The image illustrates root canal therapy, specifically the stage of "Removing Infected Pulp" as labeled. Pathologically, a yellowish periapical lesion (indicating an abscess or localized infection) is visible at the apex of the left root being treated by the file.
judge1_notes: Accurate description covering diagram type, root canal procedure, rubber dam, and periapical lesion.

description: This is a cross-sectional medical illustration depicting a root canal procedure, specifically the step labeled "Removing Infected Pulp." Visible anatomy includes a tooth with two roots embedded in spongy alveolar bone, surrounded by pink gingival tissue and the periodontal ligament. A green rubber dam isolates the tooth, and a metal cleaning file (endodontic file) is inserted deep into the left root canal. The image shows pathology consistent with pulp necrosis and periapical infection; a yellowish-brown mass at the apex of the left root indicates an abscess or lesion in the surrounding bone. The file is shown mechanically debriding the infected tissue from the canal space.

description:

The visible anatomy includes a tooth with its pulp chamber and root canal, surrounded by periodontal ligament and alveolar bone. The tooth is isolated with a rubber dam, and a cleaning file is inserted into the root canal.

The condition depicted is an infected pulp, as indicated by the title "Removing Infected Pulp" at the bottom of the image. The severity of the infection is not explicitly shown but is implied to be significant enough to require removal of the infected pulp tissue.

description:
1. The image is a diagram (illustration) of a dental procedure.  
2. Visible anatomy includes a tooth (with crown, root, pulp chamber, and root canals), alveolar bone, gingival tissue, a rubber dam (isolating the tooth), and an endodontic cleaning file inserted into a root canal.  
3. The clinical finding is an infected pulp (per the label "Removing Infected Pulp"), with the cleaning file being used to remove it; no specific severity beyond the procedure for infected pulp is depicted.

description: This is a schematic dental illustration showing stages of root canal treatment on a molar in cross-section. Visible structures include the crown, enamel/dentin, pulp chamber, root canals, gingiva, surrounding bone, and a periapical area at the root tip. The left panel shows a damaged/caries-affected tooth with pulp involvement and an apical lesion; the middle panels show access drilling and canal cleaning with an endodontic file; the right panel shows the canals filled/sealed and the tooth restored with a crown-like restoration. The depicted condition appears to be advanced pulpal infection with periapical involvement requiring endodontic therapy.
judge1_notes: Accurate description covering all four stages, modality, and periapical lesion; severity is not applicable for a diagram.

description:
**1. Type of image:** This is a schematic illustration/diagram (not a radiograph or clinical photo), depicting a four-stage sequence of a dental procedure on a multi-rooted tooth (molar).

**2. Anatomy and structures visible:** Each panel shows a longitudinal cross-section of a molar embedded in surrounding tissue. Visible structures include the crown with enamel, the dentin, the pulp chamber and root canals (shown in red as pulp/vascular tissue), two roots, the surrounding pink gingiva, and the underlying bone/alveolus (tan). A dental handpiece is shown in panel 1, and an endodontic file/instrument (blue handle with gray flexible tip) in panel 3.

**3. Condition and procedure depicted:** This illustrates **root canal therapy (endodontic treatment)**, likely prompted by deep caries/pulpal involvement. 
- **Panel 1:** Shows the affected tooth with a darkened/inflamed pulp chamber (reddish-brown discoloration), a coronal defect/cavity, and apical involvement (a greenish lesion at the root apex suggesting periapical pathology/abscess). The handpiece is positioned to access the chamber.
- **Panel 2:** The pulp chamber and canals have been opened and cleaned out (now appearing white/empty), with the access cavity prepared.
- **Panel 3:** An endodontic file is being inserted into a root canal for cleaning and shaping.
- **Panel 4:** The canals and chamber are filled with obturation material (brown), and a restoration/crown (purple-tinted) caps the crown.

The illustration depicts a tooth with apparent pulpal necrosis/infection and a periapical lesion being treated and restored.
judge1_notes: Accurate identification of the four-stage root canal diagram, lesion, and instruments; severity is not applicable.

description: This is a sequential, four-part anatomical illustration depicting the stages of root canal therapy. The visible anatomy includes a cross-section of a multi-rooted tooth (showing enamel, dentin, pulp chamber, and root canals), surrounding gingiva, and alveolar bone. The first panel illustrates severe pathology: a deep coronal defect extending into the pulp, accompanied by pulpal inflammation (colored red) and a periapical lesion or abscess (greenish area) at the root apex. The subsequent panels show the clinical progression: a dental handpiece accessing the pulp chamber, an endodontic file cleaning and shaping the root canal, and finally, the obturation (filling) of the canals and pulp chamber, capped with a coronal restoration, resulting in the apparent resolution of the periapical lesion.
judge1_notes: Accurate four-panel description covering all stages of root canal therapy; severity is not applicable for this diagram.

description:
This is a four-panel diagram illustrating the sequential steps of a root canal treatment (endodontic therapy) on a molar tooth.

1.  **Image Type:** A medical/dental educational illustration or diagram.
2.  **Anatomy:** The images show cross-sections of a molar tooth including the enamel, dentin, pulp chamber, and root canals, surrounded by gingival tissue (gums) and alveolar bone.
3.  **Condition and Procedure:**
    *   **Panel 1 (far left):** Depicts severe pathology. Extensive dental caries (decay) has penetrated the crown, causing a fracture and infecting the pulp (shown as dark red/brown inflammation). A periapical lesion or abscess (greenish mass) is visible at the root apex, indicating infection in the surrounding bone.
    *   **Panel 2:** Shows the access preparation stage. The decayed tooth structure is removed, and a dental drill (handpiece) is positioned above the tooth to create an opening into the pulp chamber.
    *   **Panel 3:** Illustrates canal instrumentation. An endodontic file (blue handle) is inserted into the root canal to clean, shape, and remove the infected tissue.
    *   **Panel 4 (far right):** Shows the final restoration. The root canals and pulp chamber are obturated (filled) with a brown material (likely gutta-percha), and the tooth is sealed with a final coronal restoration (purple/blue crown). The periapical lesion appears to have resolved or is healing.
judge1_notes: Accurate description covering all four endodontic stages, periapical lesion, and diagram modality; severity is not applicable here.

description:
The image is a diagram illustrating the steps of a root canal treatment. 

The visible anatomy includes a tooth with its enamel, dentin, pulp chamber, root canal, and surrounding periodontal tissues. The diagram shows four stages: the initial stage with a deep carious lesion extending into the pulp, followed by access cavity preparation and removal of infected pulp tissue, then cleaning and shaping of the root canal, and finally obturation of the canal and restoration of the tooth.

A severe condition is depicted, namely deep caries with pulpal involvement and periapical pathology, likely an abscess or granuloma, indicating the need for root canal treatment.
judge1_notes: Accurate four-stage RCT description covering all required points; severity is not applicable for a diagram.

description:

description: This is a labeled anatomical/histologic illustration in longitudinal section of a single-rooted tooth within its alveolar socket. Visible structures include the crown/root with outer enamel or cementum, underlying dentin, a central pulp chamber/root canal marked “c,” periodontal ligament space, gingival/soft-tissue attachment, and surrounding trabecular alveolar bone with marrow/vascular spaces. No obvious disease process, caries, fracture, or periapical pathology is shown; it appears to depict normal dental and periodontal anatomy.
